items.text: Dane pulled himself up the rough wooden siding of the barn. The shingles radiated heat???slate that had been baking under an invisible noon sun, though the hour was closer to midnight. He found a perch on the ridge and wrapped his arms around his knees.

items.text: The world we live in is not solid ground; it is a temporary structure, a graft, a scaffolding built on the impossible physics of a child who shouldn't have been born.

items.text: Bliss looked at the paper register. The stylus should have been pressing dots and dashes into the tape, compressing the fibers without breaking them. The stylus was glowing cherry red. It wasn't pressing. It was branding. The tip wasn't pressing anymore. It was eating. Each strike devoured the fiber beneath it, leaving craters rimmed with ash.

items.text: In eighteen ninety-nine, Tesla had conducted the Colorado Springs experiments. He had pumped millions of volts into the earth's crust. He had created standing waves of energy that circled the globe, bouncing off the antipodes and returning to the source. He had proven that the earth itself could be used as a conductor.
items.title: Ep1 - historical_fact

items.text: George Bliss was an employee of the Western Union Telegraph Company.

items.text: Dane stepped between the window and the crib. His body blocked the light. The geometry faltered. The white beam fractured around his silhouette, unable to pass through. The glass-like shape stuttered, lost its rhythm, pulsed erratically???three-two-five, the pattern broken.

items.text: The wire caused George Bliss's fingerprints and identity to be overwritten.

items.text: Then, at eleven-eighteen, the pattern breaks. The paper is no longer indented. It is branded. A black line drags for six inches???a continuous wound where the stylus, superheated by the surge rolling down from the aurora, stopped recording and started destroying.
